common_name: Old German Tomato (Mennonite Bicolor Yellow-Red Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Old German is believed to have originated with Mennonite German immigrant communities in Virginia and been preserved by Mennonite seed-saving traditions before entering the American heirloom seed trade.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Old German is an heirloom beefsteak tomato of Mennonite German ancestry — producing very large (1–2 lb), deeply flattened, lobed beefsteak fruits with a striking bicolor pattern of golden-yellow skin marked with deep-red and orange streaks, marbling, and patches distributed across the fruit surface in irregular patterns. The cut interior reveals the characteristic bicolor flesh of this German heirloom tradition: yellow and red sectors arranged in a beautiful mosaic pattern that is outstanding in sliced fresh presentations — the multicolored cross-section of Old German makes it one of the most visually beautiful tomatoes when displayed on a cutting board or serving plate. The flavor follows the pattern of the best bicolor heirlooms: very sweet, extremely low-acid, with a complex, fruity, mild quality that makes it exceptional for fresh eating. Old German is most clearly distinguished from similar bicolor heirlooms (Pineapple, Hillbilly, Big Rainbow) by the specific distribution and proportion of its red-and-yellow coloring — the exact pattern varies somewhat from fruit to fruit but consistently produces the beautiful bicolor marbled effect. An open-pollinated heirloom with saveable seed. In zone 9, grow as a spring crop for best results before summer heat.

common_name: White Cloud Texas Sage (White-Flowered Cenizo Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to dry, alkaline to neutral (pH 6.5–8.0), lean soil in full sun; White Cloud absolutely requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate standing water or heavy wet clay soil; plant in raised beds or berms in the humid eastern Gulf South.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; White Cloud is the white-flowered cultivar of Texas sage (cenizo, Leucophyllum frutescens) — producing the same outstanding silver-gray, small, soft, woolly foliage and the remarkable humidity-triggered synchronized blooming behavior as the purple-flowered species, but with pure white flowers that create an entirely different aesthetic. The white-flowered display is particularly striking against the silver-gray foliage — an all-silver-and-white combination that has a cool, refined quality in contrast to the warm purple-on-gray of standard cenizo cultivars. White Cloud's bloom response to humidity or rainfall (a sudden mass flowering of white across the entire shrub following a rise in relative humidity or rain event) is one of the most dramatic natural phenomena in southwestern ornamental horticulture. The white flowers pair beautifully with blue-purple companion plants (blue salvias, Russian sage) for a traditional blue-and-white planting combination, and with ornamental grasses whose warm golden tones provide contrast. Like all Leucophyllum, White Cloud requires excellent drainage and lean soil — excessive water and fertility produce soft growth and reduce the tight, silver-gray character of the natural plant. In zone 9 west of the high-humidity coastal zone (San Antonio area, central Texas), White Cloud is exceptionally hardy and reliable; in the humid Gulf Coast proper, good drainage is essential.

common_name: Mississippi Cream Pea (Traditional Gulf South Small-Seeded Cream Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mississippi Cream peas can be planted in succession every 3–4 weeks from April through August for continuous summer production — they thrive in the summer heat that limits most other garden vegetables.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mississippi Cream is a traditional Gulf South cream pea variety — producing small to medium-sized, cream-white seeds with the mild, sweet, nutty flavor characteristic of cream peas that distinguishes them from the earthier, bolder flavor of blackeye peas and cowpeas. Mississippi Cream is a semi-bush type with good productivity in Gulf South conditions and excellent heat tolerance — continuing to produce through the summer heat of zone 9 when most cool-season vegetables have ended. As a cream pea type, Mississippi Cream has slightly smaller seeds and a sweeter, more delicate flavor than standard crowder or blackeye types — the creamier texture and milder flavor of cream peas generally makes them preferred for certain traditional preparations including fresh summer pea succotash, cream peas and okra, and fresh peas braised with ham or fatback. Southern peas as a group are the most important summer legume vegetable of Gulf Coast traditional gardens — providing fresh protein through the hottest months when other vegetables fail. The nitrogen-fixing root system enriches garden soil for subsequent crops. Harvest pods at the green-shell stage (well-filled hulls but still green) for fresh eating or continue to dry maturity for storage.

common_name: Blue Max Collards (Large Upright High-Yield Smooth-Leaf Collard Hybrid)

planting.note: Direct sow 10–12 weeks before the first frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Blue Max is a hybrid collard — it will not come true from saved seed but produces more uniform plants and potentially higher yields than open-pollinated varieties when purchased as fresh hybrid seed each season.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Blue Max is a hybrid collard variety valued for its large, smooth, blue-green, broadly rounded leaves and high-yield production — producing some of the largest, most productive collard plants available for home garden and small-farm production. The large leaf size (leaves reaching 18–24 inches across on well-fed plants) and tall, upright habit produce an impressive harvest volume from each plant. As a hybrid variety, Blue Max produces uniform, vigorous plants with excellent disease tolerance and the high-yield characteristics that result from hybrid vigor. The smooth, thick, waxy leaf surface holds up well in cooking and has good culinary quality in traditional preparations — braised with smoked meats, in Southern-style stewed greens, and in other long-cooked preparations where the thick leaves benefit from extended cooking time. Blue Max is cold-hardy and continues producing through Gulf South winters, sweetening after frost exposure as starches convert to sugars — the post-frost harvest typically produces the finest-flavored greens. In zone 9, plant in August–September for fall/winter production and harvest outer leaves progressively through November–February. The very large leaves and tall plants provide ample harvest from relatively few plants in a garden bed.

common_name: Early Prolific Straightneck Squash (Classic American Yellow Summer Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; in zone 9, plant spring crops in February–March and fall crops in August–September; summer squash is susceptible to squash vine borers in zone 9 — the fall crop planted in August often misses peak vine borer pressure and may produce more reliably than the spring planting.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Early Prolific Straightneck is the classic American yellow summer squash — the standard straightneck type that has defined the yellow summer squash category in American home gardens and commercial production for decades. The smooth, straight, medium-yellow to golden-yellow squash (6–8 inches at ideal harvest) has the classic straight-neck shape (no bulge or curve at the blossom end) that distinguishes straightneck from crookneck types. The tender, mild-flavored flesh is excellent sautéed, grilled, steamed, stuffed, and in casseroles — the standard all-purpose yellow summer squash for every culinary application. An All-America Selections winner recognized for its exceptional productive performance and consistent quality. Harvest squash when small to medium (6–8 inches) for best flavor and tenderness — oversized squash develop tough skin, large hard seeds, and coarse, watery flesh. In zone 9, summer squash is best grown in spring (February–April) and fall (August–October) — the summer heat accelerates production to the point of difficulty managing harvests. The squash vine borer is the primary pest challenge in zone 9; fall planting (August) often escapes the peak infestation period. Extremely productive — a few plants provide more squash than most families can use during peak season; succession planting at 2–3 week intervals provides more manageable quantities.
